controls.controlName: Establish HAZOP Schedule and Leadership Commitment
controls.controlStatement: Who: HEMP Process Owner | What: Establish a schedule for HAZOP studies and obtain leadership approval to allocate resources | When: At the beginning of asset lifecycle and annually | Where: Asset management system and HEMP plan | Why: To ensure compliance with 5-year revalidation requirement and prevent gaps in process safety management | How: Document the schedule in HEMP Plan, identify units with RAM Red/Yellow 5A/5B risks, obtain leadership handshake on resource allocation | Evidence: Signed HEMP Plan, resource allocation statement, HAZOP schedule document
keyActivities: Identify all assets/units with RAM Red or Yellow 5A/5B process safety risks
Schedule HAZOP studies to meet 5-year revalidation requirement
Obtain leadership approval and commitment for resources
Allocate budget and personnel for HAZOP execution
Document timeline with key dates for planning and completion
controls.riskAddressed: Non-compliance with 5-year process hazard analysis requirement; loss of clarity on current process safety risks
controls.testingApproach: Verify HEMP Plan exists and is current; confirm leadership approval signature/email; validate that all units due for HAZOP are identified with scheduled dates

controls.subProcess: Gather Technical Information and History of the Unit
controls.controlName: Collect and Review Process Safety Information
controls.controlStatement: Who: HAZOP facilitator and HEMP Coordinator | What: Gather and review all technical documentation, MOC records, maintenance status, and operational history | When: 9-12 months before HAZOP workshop | Where: Asset documentation system, GSAP/CGSAP maintenance records, MOC database | Why: To ensure HAZOP study is based on current, accurate information and identifies recent changes that may introduce new hazards | How: Conduct systematic review of PSI, MOC documents since last HAZOP, preventive/corrective maintenance status, SIF PFD data, previous assurance findings | Evidence: Checklist of gathered documents, MOC summary log, maintenance status report, notes from document review meetings
keyActivities: Collect all technical design documents and P&ID updates
Review MOC documents opened/closed since last HAZOP study
Compile DOW/IOW exceedance records for the unit
Gather preventive and corrective maintenance status
Extract SIF actual PFD and demand rate data from testing results
controls.riskAddressed: HAZOP based on outdated or incomplete information; failure to identify hazards introduced by changes; gaps in understanding of barrier reliability
controls.testingApproach: Verify document checklist is complete; confirm all MOCs since last HAZOP are reviewed; validate maintenance status reflects current condition; review PFD records are recent and valid

controls.controlName: Approve HAZOP Approach Based on Risk Assessment Criteria
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2 or Business Approved HEMP SME with Asset disciplines (Operations, Process Engineering) | What: Evaluate and approve the HAZOP approach (New, Update, or Sample Review) based on documented criteria | When: Early in planning cycle, minimum 12 months before HAZOP execution | Where: HEMP Plan, HAZOP approach decision document | Why: To ensure appropriate level of HAZOP scrutiny matches the risk profile and change history of the unit | How: Apply evaluation criteria per Appendix B (robustness of previous HAZOP, MOC process effectiveness, barrier validity status); document decision rationale; obtain multi-disciplinary team approval | Evidence: HAZOP Approach Decision Document with evaluation scoring, TA2/SME approval, documented justification for approach selection
keyActivities: Assess robustness of previous 5-year HAZOP study quality
Evaluate effectiveness and documentation quality of MOC process
Review HSSE Critical Equipment and Human Barrier validity status
Assess impact of regulatory or standard changes since last HAZOP
Compare findings to criteria matrix and select appropriate HAZOP approach
controls.riskAddressed: Inappropriate HAZOP scope selection; wasted resources on unnecessary full updates; missed hazards due to insufficient review depth
controls.testingApproach: Verify evaluation criteria checklist completed; confirm TA2 or SME signature on approach decision; validate selected approach aligns with documented risk factors

controls.processArea: AIPSM Management of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers
controls.subProcess: Identification of HSSE Critical Equipment and Human Barriers
controls.controlName: Identify HSSE Critical Equipment and HSSE Critical Human Barriers from Hazard Analysis
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Manager, HEMP Focal Point, Hazard Analysis Facilitator | What: Review hazard analysis to identify equipment and human barriers critical to managing RED/YELLOW 5A/5B process safety risks; compile preliminary list | When: Following completion or update of Hazard Analysis | Where: Hazard Analysis documentation, HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers list | Why: To identify which barriers require special attention and management focus due to their role in preventing major accidents | How: Systematically review each identified scenario; identify valid barriers; determine if barrier is equipment (HSSE CE) or human action (HSSE Critical HB); document justification for inclusion; assess practicality of barrier implementation | Evidence: Compiled preliminary list of HSSE CE and HSSE Critical HB with process safety justification, identified barriers documented with scenario linkage
keyActivities: Review Hazard Analysis results for all RED/YELLOW 5A/5B scenarios
Identify all valid barriers for each scenario
Classify barriers as HSSE Critical Equipment or Human Barriers
Document justification for HSSE Critical classification
controls.riskAddressed: Critical barriers not identified; barriers misclassified; unreliable or impractical barriers designated as HSSE Critical; management focus on wrong controls
controls.testingApproach: Review preliminary list for completeness against scenarios; verify each barrier is linked to at least one RED/YELLOW scenario; confirm classification (CE vs HB) is appropriate; validate justification for inclusion

controls.processArea: AIPSM Management of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers
controls.subProcess: Assurance of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers List
controls.controlName: Conduct Technical Authority Assurance of HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers Identification
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2 or equivalent authority | What: Independently review and provide assurance on the completeness and correctness of the HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers list | When: After completion of Step 1, before management handshake | Where: Technical assurance checklist, HSSE Critical Equipment and Barriers documentation | Why: To ensure the list is technically robust, complete, and consistent with hazard analysis findings before management approval | How: Use assurance checklist to verify each item; assess completeness against all scenarios; validate definitions and justifications; check for consistency with standards; provide findings and approval | Evidence: Completed assurance checklist, technical authority approval signature, documented findings and recommendations
keyActivities: Review list for completeness using assurance checklist
Verify each HSSE CE and HB meets definition criteria
controls.riskAddressed: Incomplete or inaccurate HSSE Critical lists proceed to management; flawed equipment or barrier identifications not caught; loss of credibility with assurance bodies
controls.testingApproach: Verify TA2/Authority conducted review; review assurance checklist for completeness; confirm approval signature present; validate any recommended modifications addressed

controls.controlName: Gather AIPSM Performance Data and Leading Indicators for Management Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Unit Manager, Process Engineer, Data Analyst | What: Systematically collect and compile AIPSM performance data including design, technical, operational, and leadership integrity metrics | When: Quarterly at minimum for production unit level; annually for asset and business levels | Where: AIPSM data dashboard, reporting system, performance records | Why: To provide leadership with current visibility of barrier health and identify trends requiring attention or intervention | How: Collect data from multiple sources (maintenance system, incident reports, KPI dashboards, assurance findings); compile into visual format (heat maps, metrics); trend data over time; identify outliers or concerning trends; prepare summary for management review | Evidence: Completed AIPSM performance data reports, data trending analysis, visual dashboards showing current status and historical trends
keyActivities: Collect leading and lagging indicator data
Compile barrier health metrics from maintenance and operational data
Trend indicators over time to identify patterns
Create visual representations (heat maps, charts) of performance
Identify metrics outside target range for discussion
controls.riskAddressed: Leadership lacks current visibility of barrier health; trends in performance not detected; management decisions made without complete information
controls.testingApproach: Verify data collected from authoritative sources; confirm all required indicators included; review data compilation for accuracy; validate trends are correctly calculated

controls.controlName: Conduct Asset-Level Annual AIPSM Effectiveness Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership Team (technical, operations, process safety), relevant TA2 disciplines, HSSE Lead | What: Conduct comprehensive annual review of asset integrity to verify process safety risks remain managed to ALARP and identify improvement actions | When: Annually as part of asset management cycle | Where: Management review meeting, documented review report | Why: To systematically assess whether AIPSM program is effective and identify systemic improvements needed | How: Review process safety leading/lagging indicators and trends; assess barrier health status; review risk action management; examine incident investigations and learnings; assess technical integrity of key equipment; review competency status; identify improvement themes and priorities; document findings and actions | Evidence: Documented AIPSM review report with findings and action items, management team attendance records, tracked closure of action items
keyActivities: Review all process safety leading and lagging indicators
Assess barrier health across design, technical, operational, and leadership petals
Review risk-related action management from various sources
Examine top process safety scenarios and control status
Identify improvement actions and assign accountability
Track action closure through management system
controls.riskAddressed: AIPSM program effectiveness not assessed; systemic weaknesses not identified; improvement opportunities missed; management unaware of changing risk profile
controls.testingApproach: Verify review was conducted with appropriate team; review documented findings; confirm action items assigned with clear accountability; check tracking system for action closure

controls.controlName: Identify Events Requiring Statement of Fitness Review
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Unit Manager, Operations Supervisor, HSSE Lead | What: Identify when significant abnormal events occur that require a Statement of Fitness review (uncontrolled shutdowns, turnarounds, equipment constraint exceedances, environmental incidents) | When: Immediately when abnormal event occurs or is anticipated | Where: Event log, management communications | Why: To ensure structured technical review is conducted before facility restart; to verify controls remain effective after disruption | How: Establish clear criteria in local procedure for what constitutes a SoF event; train personnel to recognize and report such events; document event identification and decision to conduct SoF review | Evidence: Event notification, documented identification of SoF trigger event, decision record
keyActivities: Define SoF trigger criteria in local procedure
Establish communication process for event notification
Document event identification and SoF decision
controls.riskAddressed: Abnormal events not recognized as requiring SoF review; facility restarted without proper assessment; previously valid controls compromised undetected
controls.testingApproach: Verify local SoF procedure exists with clear trigger criteria; confirm training records show personnel trained; review event logs for proper identification and documentation

controls.controlName: Conduct Structured Statement of Fitness Review and Document Findings
controls.controlStatement: Who: Multi-disciplined team (Operations, Process Engineering, Discipline Engineers, TA2, HSSE) led by SoF Review Leader | What: Conduct comprehensive technical review of facility following abnormal event; assess whether controls remain adequate to manage risks to ALARP; identify any needed repairs, modifications, or interim measures | When: Prior to facility restart | Where: SoF review meeting, SoF form, supporting documentation | Why: To provide documented assurance that process safety risks are adequately managed before resuming operations | How: Investigate event root cause; identify any equipment damage or parameter exceedances; assess impact on barriers and controls; conduct risk assessment for identified impacts; define any needed repairs or modifications; verify procedures and training in place; complete SoF form with multi-disciplinary signatures | Evidence: Completed SoF form with all reviews documented, incident investigation report, risk assessment documentation, repair/modification work orders, training and procedure updates
keyActivities: Conduct root cause investigation of abnormal event
Assess equipment and system damage or degradation
Review impact on process safety barriers and controls
Conduct risk assessment for any identified impacts
Define required repairs, modifications, or interim measures
Verify procedures and training updated for restart
controls.riskAddressed: Facility restarted with inadequate controls after upset; hidden damage not identified; barriers compromised; new risks introduced during recovery
controls.testingApproach: Review SoF form for completeness of all required sections; verify incident investigation documented; confirm risk assessment completed; check that repairs/modifications verified or tracked; validate procedures updated and training completed

controls.controlName: Identify and Rate Hazards Using Risk Assessment Matrix
controls.controlStatement: Who: Hazard identification team, process safety SME | What: Identify all hazards and rate residual risk using Shell HSSE & SP Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM); document in Hazards and Effects Register | When: During initial hazard identification and updated through MOC and learning processes | Where: Hazards and Effects Register (HER), RAM documentation | Why: To establish baseline understanding of risk magnitude; to determine level of ALARP documentation effort required | How: Systematically identify hazards; assess severity and probability of consequences; apply RAM to obtain rating; document rating rationale; update HER with current ratings | Evidence: Complete HER with all hazards and risk ratings documented, RAM application documentation showing assessment methodology
keyActivities: Apply RAM to determine risk rating
controls.riskAddressed: Hazards not identified; risk ratings incorrect; inappropriate control effort allocated; ALARP documentation insufficient for risk magnitude
controls.testingApproach: Review HER for completeness of hazard identification; verify RAM ratings are correctly assigned; confirm rating rationale documented; check HER updated with current information

controls.controlName: Document ALARP Demonstration for RED and YELLOW 5A/5B Scenarios
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Safety TA2, Engineering Discipline Leads, Risk Assessment Team | What: Prepare comprehensive documented justification that RED and YELLOW 5A/5B risks are managed to ALARP; explain standards applied, alternatives considered, and barriers implemented | When: Following hazard analysis; reviewed periodically or when significant changes occur | Where: ALARP Demonstration document, project HSSE Case or asset safety documentation | Why: To provide regulatory and business justification that all reasonable measures have been taken to reduce risk; to satisfy legal obligations for major accident hazard management | How: Identify applicable standards and good practices; document how each scenario addresses standards; describe alternatives considered and reason for rejection; explain barriers selected and reliability basis; document cost-benefit analysis where applicable; obtain technical approval | Evidence: Comprehensive ALARP Demonstration document covering each RED/YELLOW scenario, cited standards and practices, cost-benefit analysis, technical approval signatures
keyActivities: Identify applicable standards and good practices for each scenario
Document how scenario controls address standards
Perform cost-benefit analysis for major controls
controls.riskAddressed: ALARP cannot be justified; regulatory non-compliance; gaps in risk reduction logic; inadequate cost-benefit analysis; controls not substantiated
controls.testingApproach: Review ALARP documentation for each RED/YELLOW scenario; verify standards cited are applicable; confirm alternatives discussion includes rejection rationale; validate cost-benefit analysis performed; check technical approval present

controls.processArea: Chemical and Outside Supply Points Assurance
controls.controlName: Categorize Outside Supply Point Risk Level Based on Assessment Criteria
controls.controlStatement: Who: OSP Authorized HSSE Lead, OSP Assessment Team | What: Evaluate OSP using risk categorization model to classify as 'Baseline' or 'High Attention' based on 10 criteria (transport mode, product hazard, location, operational complexity) | When: During prequalification; re-evaluated every 3 years | Where: OSP Assessment Platform, OSP risk categorization documentation | Why: To ensure appropriate level of assurance and contract management rigor is applied based on risk exposure | How: Score OSP against criteria (vessel/barge transport, high-risk products, flammable, blending, heating, waterway proximity, residential area, supply chain impact); apply SME judgment factor; determine categorization threshold (score <14 = Baseline, >=14 = High Attention) | Evidence: Completed risk categorization scoring sheet, categorization determination document, documented SME judgment rationale if applied
keyActivities: Assess transport modes used for product transfer
Assess geographic risk factors (waterway, residential proximity)
controls.riskAddressed: OSP risk level underestimated; inadequate assurance applied; Baseline process applied to High Attention OSP; overly stringent requirements on low-risk OSP
controls.testingApproach: Verify all 10 criteria scored; confirm scores logically support findings; validate categorization threshold correctly applied; review SME judgment documentation if score adjusted

controls.processArea: Chemical and Outside Supply Points Assurance
controls.controlName: Assess OSP Suitability for Intended Use Through Technical Evaluation
controls.controlStatement: Who: OSP Authorized HSSE Lead with assessor(s) | What: Evaluate OSP against HSSE requirements to determine if suitable for intended product storage and operations; conduct desktop assessment for Baseline OSPs and site visit for High Attention | When: Prior to contract award; re-evaluated every 3 years | Where: OSP Assessment Platform, suitability assessment report | Why: To verify OSP has capability and controls to safely manage Shell products and operations | How: For Baseline: desk review of SAQ and HSSE metrics; For High Attention: conduct site visit with SMEs; assess compliance with requirements; identify gaps and needed actions; document suitability determination (Suitable, Suitable with Comments, or Unsuitable) | Evidence: Completed assessment documentation, site visit report if conducted, suitability determination with supporting rationale, gap closure action list
keyActivities: Conduct site visit for High Attention OSPs
Define gap closure actions with timeline
controls.riskAddressed: Unsuitable OSP selected; facility lacks required safeguards; gaps not identified before contract; liability and incident risk
controls.testingApproach: Verify appropriate assessment method applied per risk category; review assessment documentation completeness; confirm suitability outcome justified by findings; check gap closure actions defined with timelines

controls.processArea: Chemical and Outside Supply Points Assurance
controls.controlName: Conduct Contract Performance Reviews at Defined Frequency
controls.controlStatement: Who: OSP Contract Holder, OSP Authorized HSSE Lead, OSP Contract Owner | What: Conduct periodic performance review meetings to assess HSSE KPIs, incident trends, audit actions, and contract compliance | When: Minimum annually for Baseline OSPs; minimum semi-annually (one onsite) for High Attention OSPs | Where: OSP facility or virtual meeting, contract performance review meeting minutes | Why: To verify OSP continues to meet HSSE requirements; to identify performance trends and areas needing improvement | How: Prepare structured agenda; review HSSE KPIs and trends; discuss incidents and learning; review audit actions and closure status; discuss any facility changes; document meeting minutes and action items | Evidence: Contract performance review meeting minutes, HSSE KPI data reviewed, attendance records, action item tracking
keyActivities: Schedule and conduct CPR at required frequency
Review HSSE KPIs and performance metrics
Review audit actions and closure status
Discuss contract changes and facility modifications
controls.riskAddressed: OSP performance decline not detected; HSSE issues not addressed; contract changes not evaluated; assurance gaps after initial assessment
controls.testingApproach: Verify CPR conducted at required frequency; review meeting minutes for required topics; confirm HSSE KPI data included; check that action items tracked and closed

controls.controlName: Develop Corrective Actions from Root Causes and Track to Closure
controls.controlStatement: Who: Investigation Team, Action Owner, Investigation Sponsor | What: Develop corrective actions addressing identified root causes; assign ownership; establish closure criteria; track to closure with evidence of effectiveness | When: Following root cause identification; actions closed based on complexity and urgency | Where: Incident investigation report, action tracking system, action closure documentation | Why: To ensure incident learnings are translated into organizational improvements that prevent recurrence | How: For each root cause, develop action addressing the cause (not just symptom); assign to competent owner with resources; define clear success criteria; establish completion timeline; implement action; verify effectiveness; document closure with evidence | Evidence: Action items linked to root causes, action owner assignment, completion evidence demonstrating effectiveness, closed action documentation
keyActivities: Develop actions for each root cause identified
Define success criteria for each action
controls.riskAddressed: Root causes not addressed; superficial actions that don't prevent recurrence; overdue actions; similar incidents recur; learning not captured
controls.testingApproach: Verify actions exist for each identified root cause; review action assignment and owner credentials; confirm success criteria documented; check implementation evidence; validate closure documentation with effectiveness verification

controls.controlName: Share Incident Learnings Across Organization
controls.controlStatement: Who: Investigation Sponsor, Process Safety Lead, HSSE Communications | What: Develop and execute plan to share incident learnings and corrective actions across relevant business units and asset communities | When: Following completion of investigation; within 30-60 days of closure | Where: Learning from Incidents (LFI) system, team briefings, newsletters, management meetings | Why: To prevent similar incidents at other assets by disseminating lessons learned; to reinforce organizational learning culture | How: Identify similar hazards/processes at other assets; prepare clear summary of incident, causes, and actions; brief operations teams; post to LFI system; include in management briefings; track awareness and implementation | Evidence: LFI system entries, briefing attendance records, dissemination plan confirmation, tracking of learning implementation at other locations
keyActivities: Identify similar processes at other assets
Prepare clear learning summary and briefing materials
Include in management reviews and communications
Track awareness and application of learnings
controls.riskAddressed: Similar incidents occur at other assets; learning not shared; organizational capability not improved; duplicate incident investigation costs
controls.testingApproach: Verify learning posted to LFI system; confirm briefings conducted at appropriate locations; review dissemination records; check tracking of learning implementation

controls.controlName: Conduct Periodic Business Continuity Plan Tests and Exercises
controls.controlStatement: Who: BCM Coordinator, Business Process Owners, Recovery Team Members | What: Execute planned tests and exercises of business continuity plans to validate procedures, verify recovery capability, and assess team readiness | When: Annually at minimum; more frequently for critical systems | Where: Test/exercise venue (desktop, simulation, or full deployment scenario) | Why: To verify recovery procedures will work as intended; to identify gaps or weaknesses; to build team confidence and competency in recovery actions | How: Develop test plan with scenarios; brief participants; execute test (tabletop, simulation, or live recovery); observe and document issues; conduct after-action review; document findings and recommendations; implement improvements | Evidence: Test plan and scenario definition, exercise execution records, observation notes and findings, after-action review documentation, improvement action tracking
controls.riskAddressed: Recovery procedures untested and unreliable; team unprepared for actual recovery; critical gaps not discovered until real incident; recovery fails or is delayed
controls.testingApproach: Verify annual test/exercise conducted; review test plan and scenario definition; check observation and after-action review documentation; confirm improvement actions implemented and tracked

controls.controlName: Maintain Current Business Continuity Plans and Update for Changes
controls.controlStatement: Who: BCM Coordinator with Business Process Owners | What: Keep BC plans current and relevant by updating when business changes occur (staff changes, system modifications, contact information, process changes) | When: Upon any significant business change; minimum annual review | Where: BC Plan documentation, change control system | Why: To ensure recovery procedures remain valid and achievable with current business configuration; outdated plans may fail during actual incident | How: Establish change process to flag updates needed; periodically review plans for relevance; update contact lists, procedures, and recovery steps reflecting current state; communicate updates to recovery teams; maintain version control | Evidence: Updated BC plan documentation with version control, change log documenting updates made, distributed updated plans to recovery teams
keyActivities: Establish change process and triggers for BC plan updates
Periodically review plans for continued relevance
Update contact lists and key information
Revise procedures based on process changes
Update recovery step sequences as needed
controls.riskAddressed: Outdated procedures in BC plans; wrong contacts or unavailable resources; recovery steps no longer valid; plans fail during execution
controls.testingApproach: Review BC plan for current dates and version control; verify change log documents recent updates; confirm contact lists are current; check procedures reflect actual business processes

controls.subProcess: Permit to Work and Execution Controls
controls.controlName: Execute Tank Cleaning Work Under Permit Control with Continuous Monitoring
controls.controlStatement: Who: Tank Cleaning Crew, Work Supervisor, Entry Attendant, Gas Tester | What: Execute tank cleaning work under controlled permit system with continuous atmospheric monitoring, ventilation, and rescue readiness | When: During planned tank cleaning activity | Where: Tank location, entry point, work area | Why: To prevent incidents from hazardous atmospheres, physical hazards, or inadequate controls during cleaning operation | How: Issue and sign work permit; verify all controls in place before work starts; maintain continuous ventilation and atmospheric monitoring; keep entry attendant stationed; ensure rescue equipment and team ready; execute cleaning per approved procedure; monitor work continuously for changing conditions | Evidence: Signed work permit, continuous atmospheric monitoring logs with readings, ventilation system logs, entry attendant presence verification, rescue team availability confirmation
keyActivities: Issue work permit with all controls specified
Verify controls in place before work commences
Verify rescue team present and ready
controls.riskAddressed: Work proceeds without control; hazardous atmosphere accumulates; personnel overcome or injured; inadequate rescue readiness; unplanned exposures
controls.testingApproach: Verify work permit issued and signed; check atmospheric monitoring logs for continuous readings; confirm ventilation operating throughout; validate entry attendant presence; confirm rescue team briefing and readiness

controls.controlName: Conduct Final Inspection and Verification Before Tank Return to Service
controls.controlStatement: Who: Tank Inspector, Facility Manager, Technical Authority | What: Conduct final inspection of cleaned tank; verify cleaning completeness; test for residual hazards; approve return to service | When: Upon completion of cleaning; before tank is returned to normal service | Where: Tank interior, inspection documentation | Why: To ensure tank is clean and safe for operation; to identify any cleaning deficiencies or emerging hazards | How: Visually inspect tank interior for residual material or contamination; test atmosphere if required; verify all isolation and tagging removed; check for structural damage from cleaning; document inspection results; approve or require rework | Evidence: Tank inspection record with completion sign-off, atmospheric test results if applicable, photographic documentation if significant residue found, approval to return to service
keyActivities: Conduct visual inspection of tank interior
Verify all debris and residual product removed
Test for hazardous atmospheres if required
controls.riskAddressed: Incomplete cleaning not detected; residual hazard material remains; contaminated product produced; operation of structurally damaged tank
controls.testingApproach: Review tank inspection record for completeness; verify inspection conducted by authorized personnel; confirm test results documented; check approval documentation

controls.controlName: Perform Periodic Maintenance and Testing of Electrical Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Qualified Electrician, Equipment Inspector | What: Conduct preventive maintenance and periodic testing of electrical equipment including voltage testing, insulation resistance, thermal imaging, and functional verification | When: Per risk-based maintenance schedule; typically annually to 5-yearly depending on equipment type and criticality | Where: Equipment location, maintenance records | Why: To detect degradation before failure; to prevent electrical hazards (shock, arc flash, fire) from failed equipment | How: Develop maintenance schedule based on equipment type and manufacturer recommendations; perform maintenance per procedure (cleaning, lubrication, fastener tightening); conduct electrical tests (insulation, load testing); document results and any defects found; perform corrective maintenance if needed before return to service | Evidence: Preventive maintenance work orders with completion documentation, test results with pass/fail determination, defect reports for any issues found, equipment inspection and acceptance records
controls.riskAddressed: Electrical equipment degradation undetected; equipment fails unexpectedly; electrical hazards (shock, arc flash) not identified; safety equipment non-functional
controls.testingApproach: Review maintenance schedule for completeness; verify maintenance work orders completed per schedule; review test results for proper documentation; check that defects identified and repaired

controls.controlName: Provide Driving Training and Maintain Driver Competency
controls.controlStatement: Who: Training Provider, Fleet Manager, Driver Supervisor | What: Provide initial driving training for company drivers; conduct periodic competency verification and refresher training | When: Upon hire or role change; minimum biennially for competency verification | Where: Training venue, vehicle operation environment, training records | Why: To ensure drivers have knowledge and skills to operate vehicles safely; to maintain competency and address skill drift | How: Develop training curriculum addressing defensive driving, hazard recognition, vehicle-specific operation, and company policies; conduct initial training and assessment; track competency through on-road observation and periodic formal assessment; provide refresher training when skills decline | Evidence: Training completion certificates, competency assessment records, training attendance logs, observation notes from supervisors on driver performance
keyActivities: Conduct initial training for new drivers
controls.riskAddressed: Untrained drivers operating vehicles; unsafe driving practices; vehicle incidents increase; poor hazard recognition and decision-making
controls.testingApproach: Verify training curriculum covers required topics; check training records show completion; review competency assessment records for periodicity; observe driver performance during road operation

controls.controlName: Execute Excavation Work with Continuous Monitoring and Protective Systems
controls.controlStatement: Who: Excavation Supervisor, Equipment Operator, Competent Observer | What: Oversee excavation work execution with continuous monitoring for ground stability, atmospheric conditions, and underground utilities; maintain protective systems in place | When: Throughout all excavation activity | Where: Excavation site, designated work area | Why: To prevent incidents from ground collapse, utility strikes, or confined space hazards | How: Maintain authorized person onsite to oversee work; conduct continuous visual monitoring of excavation progress and ground condition; maintain and monitor protective systems (shoring, barriers); conduct atmospheric testing if confined space risk; re-test and verify ground stability after breaks or delays; prevent unauthorized entry | Evidence: Work supervision logs documenting ongoing oversight, atmospheric test records if applicable, protective system inspection logs, incident-free work completion
keyActivities: Maintain authorized person onsite throughout work
Conduct continuous visual monitoring of excavation
Verify protective systems remain in place
controls.riskAddressed: Ground collapse during excavation; personnel in excavation struck by debris or cave-in; utility strike causes release or power loss; confined space hazards not managed
controls.testingApproach: Verify authorized person present and actively supervising; review supervision logs for continuous monitoring; check protective system inspection records; confirm atmospheric tests conducted if required

controls.controlName: Properly Backfill and Reinstate Excavation Site
controls.controlStatement: Who: Excavation Supervisor, Site Manager | What: Properly backfill excavation in layers; compact material per specifications; reinstate site to safe condition; remove temporary protective systems only after backfill complete | When: Upon completion of excavation work | Where: Excavation site, reinstatement work area | Why: To prevent ground subsidence, future tripping hazards, and loss of structural integrity | How: Backfill with specified material in compacted layers; achieve specified compaction density; remove shoring and protective equipment systematically; monitor ground for settlement after completion; verify surface reinstatement meets original grade and condition | Evidence: Backfill completion work order with material type and compaction verification, photographic documentation of reinstatement, settlement monitoring records if applicable
keyActivities: Verify final surface condition and grade
controls.riskAddressed: Inadequate backfill leads to subsidence; tripping hazards remain; structural integrity compromised; future safety issues from poor reinstatement
controls.testingApproach: Verify backfill material documented; review compaction testing records; check photographic documentation of reinstatement; confirm surface monitoring if required

controls.controlName: Monitor and Manage Contractor HSSE Performance During Execution
controls.controlStatement: Who: Contract Manager, Work Supervisor, HSSE Lead | What: Monitor contractor HSSE performance; track KPIs and incidents; conduct site observations; manage performance issues | When: Continuously throughout contract execution | Where: Contract site, performance monitoring records | Why: To ensure contractor maintains HSSE standards during work execution; to detect performance degradation; to intervene before incidents occur | How: Establish HSSE KPI targets in contract; track performance metrics (incident rates, audit findings, observation results); conduct periodic site visits observing work execution; address performance issues promptly; provide feedback and coaching; escalate if performance does not improve | Evidence: Performance monitoring records with KPI tracking, site visit observation records, performance feedback documentation, corrective action records
keyActivities: Establish HSSE KPI targets in contract
Track contractor incident and near-miss rates
Conduct periodic HSSE observations at worksite
controls.riskAddressed: Contractor HSSE performance degrades during execution; incidents not prevented; compliance slips undetected; inadequate management response to issues
controls.testingApproach: Review established KPI targets and monitoring approach; verify site observations conducted; check performance records for completeness; confirm issues documented and addressed

controls.controlName: Complete Contractor Project Close-Out and Handover
controls.controlStatement: Who: Contract Manager, Project Lead, HSSE Lead | What: Conduct final assessment of contractor work; verify contractual obligations completed; document final HSSE performance; conduct formal handover | When: Upon completion of contracted work scope | Where: Site location, project documentation, punch-list records | Why: To confirm work meets specification; to verify any defects rectified; to document final performance for contractor management | How: Conduct final inspection of work; complete punch-list of any remaining items; verify all documentation complete and delivered; conduct final HSSE review; obtain sign-off from relevant authorities; formally close contract and transition responsibilities | Evidence: Final inspection records and punch-list completion, final HSSE performance summary, contract closure documentation, formal handover confirmation
keyActivities: Conduct final inspection of completed work
Complete punch-list of any remaining items
Verify all deliverables and documentation complete
controls.riskAddressed: Work not fully complete; defects not corrected; documentation incomplete; contractor performance evaluation not documented; loose ends in transition
controls.testingApproach: Review final inspection and punch-list for completeness; verify deliverables and documentation provided; review final HSSE performance assessment; confirm contract formally closed

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Ensure Plan Delivery
controls.controlName: Develop Production Plan Within Design Operating Window Parameters
controls.controlStatement: Who: Production Planning Team, Process Engineer, Operations Manager | What: Develop production plan (rates, product slate, campaign duration) within approved Design Operating Window (DOW) parameters; obtain management approval | When: Periodically as part of production scheduling; updated for significant changes | Where: Production plan document, approved by management | Why: To ensure production operations remain within equipment design limits; to prevent equipment stress and unplanned shutdowns | How: Review current DOW parameters and any limitations; develop plan that operates within parameters; include margin for normal operating variations; obtain management review and approval; communicate approved plan to operations | Evidence: Documented production plan showing planned rates and campaigns, DOW parameter reference, management approval signature, communicated plan instructions
keyActivities: Review current Design Operating Window parameters
Develop production plan within DOW limits
controls.riskAddressed: Production planned outside DOW; equipment operated beyond design limits; unplanned shutdowns from equipment stress; barrier failure
controls.testingApproach: Verify production plan documented; confirm DOW parameters reviewed; check plan rates are within DOW limits; verify management approval obtained

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Abnormal Situations
controls.subProcess: Recognition and Response to Abnormal Conditions
controls.controlName: Identify Abnormal Operating Conditions and Execute Response Procedure
controls.controlStatement: Who: Console Operator, Control Room Personnel, Operations Supervisor | What: Identify when process parameters exceed limits or normal ranges; execute defined response procedure to bring process back to normal operation or safely shut down | When: Upon recognition of abnormal condition | Where: Control room, operating area, process control systems | Why: To prevent process upsets from escalating into safety incidents; to maintain equipment within safe operating envelope | How: Establish clear abnormal condition definitions in operating procedures; train operators on recognition criteria; define specific response actions for each type of abnormal condition; execute response per procedure; monitor effectiveness; escalate if condition does not normalize | Evidence: Alarm logs showing abnormal condition detection, operator response documentation, corrective action records, trend data showing condition resolution
keyActivities: Define abnormal conditions in operating procedures
Establish alarm setpoints for parameter limits
Train operators on recognition and response
Escalate if condition does not normalize
controls.riskAddressed: Abnormal conditions not recognized; delayed response allows escalation; wrong corrective action taken; equipment stress or upset develops
controls.testingApproach: Review operating procedure definitions of abnormal conditions; verify alarm setpoints are appropriate; observe operator response to simulated abnormal condition; check incident records for abnormal condition handling

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Abnormal Situations
controls.controlName: Investigate and Manage Design Operating Window Exceedances
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Operations Manager, HSSE Lead | What: Investigate any exceedance of Design Operating Window (DOW) parameters; assess impact on equipment integrity; implement interim measures if needed; document findings and actions | When: Immediately upon discovery of DOW exceedance | Where: Investigation documentation, equipment inspection records | Why: To assess whether equipment degradation occurred; to determine if interim safety measures needed before normal operations resume | How: Upon detection, assess magnitude and duration of exceedance; inspect affected equipment for damage; evaluate impact on barriers or integrity; determine if interim measures (reduced rates, enhanced monitoring) needed; plan corrective action; document findings in investigation report; obtain management approval for resuming normal operations | Evidence: Investigation report documenting exceedance details and impact assessment, equipment inspection records, interim measure implementation if needed, Statement of Fitness review if required before restart
keyActivities: Assess magnitude and duration of exceedance
Inspect equipment for damage or degradation
Evaluate impact on process safety barriers
Obtain approval before resuming normal operations
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment damage from exceedance not detected; operations resume with degraded equipment; barrier failure during subsequent operations
controls.testingApproach: Review investigation report for completeness; verify equipment inspection conducted; confirm impact assessment documented; check that interim measures defined if needed

controls.processArea: Ensure Safe Production - Manage Limits and Alarms
controls.controlName: Establish and Maintain Critical Alarm Setpoints
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Instrumentation Specialist, Operations Manager | What: Establish alarm setpoints for critical process parameters; base setpoints on process safety requirements and equipment design limits; document setpoint rationale; update as design changes | When: During initial system design/startup; reviewed/updated per MOC and technical reviews | Where: Alarm documentation, control system configuration, operating procedures | Why: To ensure timely detection of abnormal conditions before they escalate; to provide operators with clear indication of process boundaries | How: Identify critical parameters requiring alarms; calculate appropriate setpoints based on design limits with safety margin; configure alarms in DCS/PLC; document setpoint basis and assumptions; obtain technical review and approval; train operators on alarm meaning and response | Evidence: Documented alarm setpoints with calculation basis, engineering analysis supporting setpoint values, alarm configuration records in control system, operator training records
keyActivities: Calculate alarm setpoints with safety margin
Train operators on alarms and responses
controls.riskAddressed: Alarm setpoints incorrect; operators receive no warning of abnormal conditions; process upset escalates; equipment damage or release occurs
controls.testingApproach: Review alarm setpoint documentation for completeness; verify calculation basis provided; confirm alarms configured in control system; check operator training on alarms

controls.controlName: Establish Proactive Technical Monitoring Program
controls.controlStatement: Who: Process Engineer, Instrument Technician, HSSE Lead | What: Develop comprehensive proactive monitoring plan to detect equipment degradation before failure; include monitoring methodology, frequency, and acceptance criteria | When: During system design; updated per MOC or condition monitoring findings | Where: Proactive Monitoring Plan document, monitoring procedures | Why: To provide early warning of equipment degradation; to enable preventive action before failure occurs; to maintain integrity of safety barriers | How: Identify critical equipment requiring monitoring; select appropriate monitoring techniques (vibration, temperature, corrosion); establish monitoring frequency based on risk and failure modes; define acceptance criteria and action limits; assign monitoring responsibility; document plan | Evidence: Documented Proactive Monitoring Plan, equipment-specific monitoring procedures, monitoring acceptance criteria, assigned monitoring responsibilities
keyActivities: Define acceptance criteria and action limits
controls.riskAddressed: Equipment degradation undetected; failure occurs without warning; preventive maintenance opportunity missed; barrier failure during operation
controls.testingApproach: Review Proactive Monitoring Plan for completeness; verify critical equipment identified; check monitoring frequency and methodology appropriate; confirm acceptance criteria defined

controls.controlName: Assess Fatigue Risk from Shift Patterns and Work Schedules
controls.controlStatement: Who: Occupational Health Professional, HSSE Lead, Operations Manager | What: Assess fatigue risk associated with shift patterns, roster rotations, and work schedules; identify high-risk arrangements | When: Upon implementation of new shift pattern; periodically as part of HSSE review | Where: Fatigue risk assessment documentation, work schedule records | Why: To identify shift patterns that create elevated fatigue risk; to implement controls to manage fatigue-related incidents | How: Assess shift pattern against fatigue science principles (circadian disruption, recovery time); calculate fatigue risk index using recognized methodology; identify high-risk periods and roles; engage workers in assessment; document findings and recommendations | Evidence: Fatigue risk assessment report with methodology and findings, shift pattern documentation, identified high-risk periods, worker engagement records
keyActivities: Review shift patterns and roster rotations
Consult with workers on fatigue impacts
controls.riskAddressed: High-fatigue shift patterns continue unrecognized; workers operate while fatigued; fatigue-related incidents occur; cognitive performance degraded
controls.testingApproach: Review assessment methodology for fatigue science basis; verify shift patterns analyzed; confirm high-risk periods identified; check worker consultation occurred

controls.controlName: Implement Controls to Manage Identified Fatigue Risks
controls.controlStatement: Who: Operations Manager, Occupational Health Lead, HSSE Lead | What: Implement controls to reduce fatigue risk including shift pattern modifications, mandatory breaks, fitness for duty assessments, and fatigue awareness training | When: Upon identification of fatigue risk; integrated into ongoing scheduling | Where: Work schedules, fitness for duty procedures, training program | Why: To reduce fatigue-related incidents; to protect worker health and safety; to maintain operational performance | How: Modify shift patterns to reduce circadian disruption; ensure adequate recovery time; establish mandatory break requirements; implement pre-shift fatigue checks for safety-critical roles; provide fatigue awareness training; monitor effectiveness of controls | Evidence: Modified work schedule documentation, fitness for duty procedure, mandatory break records, fatigue training completion certificates, incident trend analysis showing fatigue risk reduction
keyActivities: Track incident trends related to fatigue
controls.riskAddressed: Fatigue risk continues despite identification; workers operate while fatigued; safety-critical decisions made by fatigued personnel; incidents escalate
controls.testingApproach: Verify shift pattern changes implemented; confirm mandatory breaks in place; observe pre-shift fatigue assessment process; review training records; check incident data for fatigue-related trends

controls.controlName: Conduct Medical Fitness Assessment for Safety-Critical Positions
controls.controlStatement: Who: Occupational Health Physician, Medical Professional | What: Conduct medical assessments to verify workers are medically fit to perform safety-critical duties; identify any medical conditions affecting fitness | When: Prior to initial assignment to safety-critical role; periodically as part of medical surveillance; upon return from leave | Where: Medical assessment office, occupational health records | Why: To ensure workers do not have medical conditions that could impair safety-critical performance; to identify need for accommodations or role modifications | How: Develop list of safety-critical roles requiring medical assessment; conduct baseline medical examination including relevant tests; assess conditions against fitness criteria; obtain occupational health professional judgment; provide medical clearance or recommend accommodations | Evidence: Medical assessment records documenting examination findings, fitness determination (fit, unfit, or fit with restrictions), any recommended accommodations, medical clearance documentation
keyActivities: Identify roles requiring medical fitness assessment
Develop assessment protocol for each role
controls.riskAddressed: Worker with medical condition impairing safety-critical judgment assigned to role; medical incapacity during critical task; preventable incidents from known conditions
controls.testingApproach: Verify medical assessments conducted for safety-critical roles; review assessment documentation for completeness; confirm fitness determination made; check that accommodations implemented if recommended

controls.controlName: Risk Assess Hot Work Activity Before Permit Issuance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Hot Work Supervisor, HSSE Lead, Authorized Person | What: Assess hot work activity (welding, grinding, cutting, etc.) for fire and explosion risks before work commences; identify required controls | When: Prior to issuance of hot work permit | Where: Hot work risk assessment documentation, hot work permit | Why: To identify specific hazards associated with hot work at each location; to ensure appropriate safeguards are in place | How: Assess surrounding environment for flammable materials; evaluate ventilation adequacy; identify potential ignition sources; assess proximity to confined spaces or utilities; specify required controls (fire watch, extinguishers, atmospheric testing); document assessment | Evidence: Completed hot work risk assessment, hazard identification for specific location, specified control measures, documented assessment rationale
keyActivities: Identify hot work equipment and process
controls.riskAddressed: Hot work performed without hazard assessment; fire or explosion risk underestimated; inadequate safeguards in place; preventable fire incidents
controls.testingApproach: Verify risk assessment conducted; review assessment document for hazard completeness; confirm control measures specified; check controls are verified in place before work begins

controls.subProcess: Hot Work Permit Issuance and Execution
controls.controlName: Issue Hot Work Permit and Maintain Fire Watch During Activity
controls.controlStatement: Who: Authorized Hot Work Permit Issuer, Fire Watch Personnel, Work Supervisor | What: Issue hot work permit documenting all required controls and precautions; maintain assigned fire watch during and for specified period after hot work | When: For all hot work activities; fire watch maintained throughout work and specified period after | Where: Hot work location, documented on permit | Why: To document authorized work and required controls; to provide immediate response capability if fire starts | How: Complete permit with location, activity, controls required, and responsible parties; ensure all controls in place before work starts; station fire watch with extinguishing equipment; maintain visual surveillance; continue watch after work stops for required period (typically 30-60 minutes); sign off permit upon completion | Evidence: Signed hot work permit with all required information, fire watch personnel assignment, fire watch observation records, post-work area inspection documentation
keyActivities: Complete hot work permit with all details
Verify all controls in place before work
Position fire watch at strategic location
controls.riskAddressed: Hot work proceeds without permit control; inadequate fire watch; fire starts with no immediate suppression; unplanned fire-related incident
controls.testingApproach: Verify hot work permit issued and properly completed; confirm fire watch present during work; observe fire watch positioning and attentiveness; check post-work area inspection completed

controls.controlName: Conduct Periodic Inspections and Maintenance of Lifting Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Competent Lifting Equipment Inspector, Equipment Engineer | What: Conduct visual inspections and load testing of cranes, hoists, slings, shackles, and other lifting equipment; verify compliance with design standards; document findings | When: Minimum annually for frequently used equipment; more often if used heavily or warning signs apparent | Where: Equipment location, inspection documentation | Why: To detect wear, damage, or degradation before equipment failure; to prevent dropped loads and associated injuries | How: Conduct visual inspection per checklist (wear, cracks, corrosion, damage); perform load testing per requirements (proof load, operational load); document measurements and findings; perform corrective maintenance if needed; issue inspection certificate when passed | Evidence: Lifting equipment inspection records with measurements and test results, equipment certification documents, maintenance records for any repairs, visual inspection checklist completion
controls.riskAddressed: Lifting equipment degradation undetected; equipment failure during lift; load drop; personnel injury or fatality
controls.testingApproach: Verify inspections conducted at required frequency; review inspection records for completeness; confirm load testing performed and documented; check repair records for defects found

controls.controlName: Plan and Execute Lifting Operations with Qualified Personnel
controls.controlStatement: Who: Lift Planner, Crane Operator, Slinger/Banksman | What: Develop comprehensive lift plan for complex lifts; ensure qualified personnel operate equipment; maintain control during lift execution | When: Prior to all lifting operations, especially for heavy or complex lifts | Where: Lift site, documented lift plan | Why: To prevent loss of control during lift; to ensure all participants understand critical points and hazards | How: Identify lift complexity; develop formal lift plan (for heavy/complex lifts) documenting equipment, methodology, weather considerations, communication signals; verify operator and personnel qualifications; brief team on plan and signals; execute with clear communication and control | Evidence: Completed lift plan documenting all critical details, equipment certification records, operator licensing/certification, pre-lift briefing records, signed work authorization
keyActivities: Assess lift complexity and required controls
Develop formal lift plan if needed
Brief team on plan and procedures
controls.riskAddressed: Unplanned lift exceeds equipment capability; unqualified personnel operate equipment; communication breakdown during lift; load dropped due to poor planning
controls.testingApproach: Verify lift plan developed for complex lifts; confirm operator licensing current; observe pre-lift briefing; check that equipment certification in place; verify communication signals understood by team

controls.controlName: Identify and Assess Threats to Asset Integrity and Performance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Engineer, Technical Authority, Subject Matter Expert | What: Systematically identify physical threats (corrosion, erosion, cracking) and operational threats (process upsets, equipment misuse) that could degrade asset integrity; assess risk magnitude | When: During asset design and updated through periodic reviews | Where: Threat assessment documentation, Risk Register | Why: To understand degradation mechanisms; to establish basis for monitoring and maintenance programs | How: Review equipment design and materials; assess environmental conditions; identify degradation modes from experience and literature; assess likelihood and consequence of each threat; prioritize by risk magnitude | Evidence: Threat assessment document identifying physical and operational threats, risk rating for each threat, monitoring strategy for high-risk threats
controls.riskAddressed: Threats to asset integrity not identified; degradation progresses undetected; equipment failure occurs; process safety barriers compromised
controls.testingApproach: Review threat assessment for completeness; verify physical and operational threats identified; confirm risk assessment conducted; check that monitoring strategy defined for high-risk threats

controls.controlName: Implement Monitoring and Mitigation for Identified Asset Threats
controls.controlStatement: Who: Equipment Engineer, Maintenance Technician, Integrity SME | What: Establish monitoring program for identified threats; implement mitigation measures (material selection, operating practices, protective coatings); track threat status | When: Integrated into asset management planning; updated per threat assessment findings | Where: Monitoring procedures, maintenance plans, equipment specifications | Why: To detect threat progression early; to prevent or delay equipment degradation | How: Select appropriate monitoring method for each threat (visual, instrumental); establish monitoring frequency; document baseline condition; track changes over time; implement preventive measures per design; adjust monitoring based on threat trends | Evidence: Threat monitoring procedures, baseline condition documentation, monitoring records showing trend data, mitigation measures implemented and verified
keyActivities: Select monitoring method for each threat
controls.riskAddressed: Threats not monitored; degradation progresses undetected; mitigation measures not implemented; equipment fails before detection
controls.testingApproach: Verify monitoring program established for high-risk threats; review monitoring procedures and frequency; check baseline and trending data collected; confirm mitigation measures implemented

controls.controlName: Identify and Assess Dropped Objects Hazards
controls.controlStatement: Who: HSSE Lead, Facility Manager, Area Supervisor | What: Systematically identify locations where dropped objects could occur and potential consequence areas; assess risk and determine control requirements | When: During facility design; updated when significant changes occur | Where: Dropped objects risk assessment, facility layout documentation | Why: To understand dropped objects risk; to implement appropriate safeguards | How: Map all elevated locations where objects could fall; identify activities and items that could be dropped; assess consequence areas below (personnel locations, critical equipment, escape routes); determine control strategy based on risk magnitude | Evidence: Dropped objects risk assessment map showing high-risk areas, identified dropped objects hazards, consequence areas identified, control strategy documented
keyActivities: Assess drop height and impact energy
controls.riskAddressed: Dropped objects hazard underestimated; consequence areas not protected; personnel in impact zone unaware of hazard; injuries from dropped objects
controls.testingApproach: Review assessment for completeness of elevated locations; verify consequence areas identified; confirm controls specified; check that personnel briefed on dropped objects hazards

controls.controlName: Implement Dropped Objects Prevention Measures and Barriers
controls.controlStatement: Who: Facility Manager, HSSE Lead, Work Supervisor | What: Implement barriers and controls to prevent objects falling from heights; maintain control systems; train personnel on dropped objects prevention | When: Integrated into facility design and operations; maintained continuously | Where: Facility locations, work procedures, personnel training | Why: To prevent dropped objects from reaching personnel below | How: Install physical barriers (netting, shields) where feasible; establish tool control procedures (tethering, tool lanyards); establish work zone controls (cordoning, warning signs); train personnel to maintain dropped objects awareness; conduct periodic inspections of control systems | Evidence: Installed dropped objects prevention measures (barriers, netting, etc.), tool control procedures and implementation records, work zone control documentation, training records on dropped objects prevention
controls.riskAddressed: Objects fall uncontrolled to areas below; personnel struck by falling objects; preventable dropped object injuries
controls.testingApproach: Verify physical barriers installed in high-risk areas; observe tool control implementation; review work zone controls in place; check training records on dropped objects prevention

controls.controlName: Capture and Document Critical Asset Knowledge
controls.controlStatement: Who: Subject Matter Experts, Equipment Engineers, Operations SMEs | What: Systematically capture critical knowledge about asset design, operation, maintenance, and history in documented form | When: Continuously as knowledge is generated; especially before SME departure | Where: Knowledge management system, asset documentation library | Why: To preserve critical knowledge; to prevent loss when personnel leave; to facilitate knowledge transfer to new team members | How: Identify critical knowledge areas (design basis, operational control strategies, equipment behavior, lessons learned); engage SMEs to document knowledge in accessible form (procedures, design documents, case studies); organize in searchable system; ensure documentation kept current | Evidence: Documented asset knowledge covering key areas, organized in accessible system, evidence of regular use and updating, search/retrieval functionality
controls.riskAddressed: Critical knowledge lost when SMEs depart; new team members lack understanding of asset; decisions made without knowledge of history
controls.testingApproach: Verify critical knowledge documented and accessible; check knowledge system in use; confirm documentation current; observe new team member access to knowledge

controls.controlName: Establish Culture of Continuous Improvement and Learning
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership, HSSE Lead, Continuous Improvement Team | What: Create organizational culture emphasizing continuous improvement and learning from experience; establish processes for capturing, analyzing, and implementing improvements | When: Integrated into asset management; ongoing throughout asset lifecycle | Where: Leadership communications, improvement meetings, documented improvements | Why: To drive sustained improvement in HSSE and operational performance; to build organizational capability | How: Establish leadership expectation for improvement; create forums for idea sharing (safety meetings, improvement circles, suggestion system); implement recognized improvement methodology; track and recognize improvements implemented; communicate learning and improvements across organization | Evidence: Documented improvement initiatives with implementation records, improvement metrics showing positive trends, team participation records in improvement activities, recognition of implemented improvements
keyActivities: Create forums for ideas and suggestions
controls.riskAddressed: Continuous improvement not pursued; opportunities for enhancement missed; organizational capability not developed; performance plateaus
controls.testingApproach: Review improvement initiatives documented; verify metrics show positive trends; observe participation in improvement activities; confirm improvements implemented and tracked

controls.controlName: Conduct Environmental and Social Impact Assessment for Project Activities
controls.controlStatement: Who: Environmental Specialist, Project Manager, Community Liaison | What: Assess potential environmental and social impacts of project activities including ecosystem effects, water quality, air emissions, employment, and community relations | When: Early in project development; updated if scope changes significantly | Where: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) report | Why: To understand potential impacts; to design mitigation measures; to meet regulatory and stakeholder requirements | How: Identify project activities and potential impact areas; assess baseline conditions; evaluate potential impacts (direct, indirect, cumulative); propose mitigation hierarchy (avoid, minimize, restore, offset); consult with stakeholders; document findings and mitigation strategy | Evidence: Completed ESIA report documenting assessment methodology, identified impacts, mitigation measures proposed, stakeholder consultation records
keyActivities: Identify project activities and potential impacts
Assess baseline environmental and social conditions
Evaluate potential impacts magnitude and extent
controls.riskAddressed: Environmental and social impacts not assessed; mitigation not planned; regulatory non-compliance; community opposition
controls.testingApproach: Review ESIA report for completeness of impact identification; verify mitigation hierarchy applied; confirm stakeholder consultation occurred; check that monitoring plan established

controls.processArea: Effective First and Second Line Assurance
controls.controlName: Establish First Line Assurance Program and Assign Responsibility
controls.controlStatement: Who: Asset Leadership, HSSE Lead, Frontline Managers | What: Establish first line assurance program with defined activities, assigned ownership, documented procedures, and communication mechanisms | When: During asset startup; reviewed and updated periodically | Where: First Line Assurance Plan, documented procedures, assurance schedules | Why: To provide continuous monitoring of control effectiveness; to identify issues and trends for rapid correction | How: Define first line assurance activities (observations, inspections, audits, data review); assign responsibility to frontline managers; establish schedules and procedures; define communication process for issues found; provide training on assurance activities; establish tracking system for actions | Evidence: Documented First Line Assurance Plan with activities and responsibilities, training records for supervisors and managers, observation/inspection records, issues tracking system
controls.riskAddressed: Controls not monitored for effectiveness; issues not detected early; problems escalate unobserved; management unaware of actual risk
controls.testingApproach: Verify First Line Assurance Plan exists and is current; review observation/inspection records; confirm responsibilities assigned; check issue tracking and closure

controls.processArea: Effective First and Second Line Assurance
controls.subProcess: Second Line Assurance Planning and Execution
controls.controlName: Conduct Second Line Assurance Reviews of HSSE Control Effectiveness
controls.controlStatement: Who: Technical Authority, Business Assurance Coordinator, Discipline-Specific Auditors | What: Conduct periodic independent assurance reviews (AIPSM audits, WPC audits) to assess whether HSSE controls are being managed and executed effectively; identify systemic issues | When: Per approved assurance plan; minimum biennial for critical systems | Where: Assurance audit records, findings reports, corrective action tracking | Why: To provide independent assessment of control effectiveness; to identify systemic weaknesses not apparent to first line | How: Develop risk-based assurance plan identifying focus areas; conduct audits using defined methodology (interviews, document review, observation); document findings with evidence; categorize findings by severity; prioritize corrective actions; track actions to closure | Evidence: Second Line Assurance Plan documenting scope and frequency, audit execution records, documented findings with evidence, corrective action tracking
controls.riskAddressed: Control effectiveness not independently assessed; systemic issues not identified; problems missed by first line oversight
controls.testingApproach: Verify assurance plan exists and is current; review audit execution records; confirm findings documented with supporting evidence; check corrective actions tracked and closed

controls.controlName: Assess Equipment Reliability and Identify Chronic Failure Issues
controls.controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Maintenance Manager, Equipment Engineer | What: Analyze equipment failure data and maintenance history to identify chronically unreliable equipment ('bad actors'); assess failure modes and contributing causes | When: Periodically (at least annually) as part of maintenance optimization; when equipment shows patterns of failure | Where: Reliability analysis reports, failure trend documentation | Why: To identify equipment requiring enhanced attention; to prioritize reliability improvement efforts | How: Compile failure history and maintenance records; analyze failure frequency and patterns; calculate Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF); identify repeat failure modes; assess root causes (design, maintenance, operation); rank equipment by reliability risk | Evidence: Reliability analysis documentation showing failure trends, identified 'bad actor' equipment, failure mode analysis, improvement prioritization
controls.riskAddressed: Chronically unreliable equipment continues with high failure rate; unplanned downtime; barrier reliability degraded
controls.testingApproach: Review reliability analysis for completeness; verify failure data analyzed; confirm bad actor equipment identified; check improvement recommendations documented

controls.controlName: Implement Reliability Improvements for Identified Problem Equipment
controls.controlStatement: Who: Reliability Engineer, Equipment Specialist, Maintenance Manager | What: Develop and implement improvement plans for identified unreliable equipment including design modifications, maintenance strategy changes, or operational controls | When: Following reliability assessment; improvements prioritized by risk and impact | Where: Equipment modification documentation, updated maintenance procedures, operational controls | Why: To improve equipment reliability and availability; to reduce unplanned downtime and maintenance costs | How: For each bad actor equipment, develop root cause-based improvement plan (design change, maintenance optimization, operational controls); implement improvement; measure effectiveness through failure trend monitoring; adjust approach based on results | Evidence: Reliability improvement plans with baseline metrics, implemented modifications or procedure changes, post-improvement failure trend data showing improvement
keyActivities: Develop improvement plan for each problem equipment
Analyze root causes and address in plan
controls.riskAddressed: Unreliable equipment continues without improvement; unplanned downtime continues; improvement opportunity missed
controls.testingApproach: Verify improvement plans developed for bad actor equipment; check modifications or procedure changes implemented; confirm post-improvement reliability monitored and shown to improve

controls.controlName: Develop Equipment Care Strategy Based on Criticality and Reliability
controls.controlStatement: Who: Equipment Engineer, Maintenance Planner, Reliability SME | What: Develop maintenance strategy for each equipment item based on criticality to production and process safety, failure modes, and reliability requirements | When: During equipment specification/selection; updated per reliability reviews | Where: Equipment Care Strategy documentation, maintenance plans | Why: To ensure maintenance effort is appropriately focused on critical and high-failure-risk equipment | How: Assess equipment criticality (impact of failure on production, safety, environment); identify failure modes and consequences; select appropriate maintenance strategy (run-to-failure, fixed-interval PM, condition-based); document strategy rationale; ensure strategy resources are available | Evidence: Documented Equipment Care Strategy for critical equipment, maintenance strategy selection rationale, allocated resources, strategy implementation plan
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance effort not focused on critical equipment; low-risk equipment over-maintained; critical equipment under-maintained; maintenance strategy inappropriate for risk
controls.testingApproach: Review equipment care strategies for appropriateness of strategy to criticality; verify resources allocated; check that critical equipment has proactive maintenance approach

controls.controlName: Execute Asset Care Plan and Monitor Performance
controls.controlStatement: Who: Maintenance Technician, Maintenance Planner, Operations Manager | What: Execute planned maintenance activities per developed strategy; track completion; monitor equipment performance and condition to verify strategy effectiveness | When: Continuously throughout asset lifecycle | Where: Maintenance system, work orders, equipment performance data | Why: To maintain equipment in operational condition; to deliver promised reliability and availability | How: Schedule maintenance per strategy; execute work per procedures; track completion and findings; collect performance data (uptime, failure rate, repair frequency); compare actual performance against strategy targets; adjust strategy if performance does not meet expectations | Evidence: Completed maintenance work orders, equipment performance data showing uptime and reliability metrics, maintenance completion rates tracking against schedule, strategy effectiveness assessment
controls.riskAddressed: Maintenance not executed per strategy; equipment performance deteriorates; reliability targets not achieved; unplanned failures occur
controls.testingApproach: Verify maintenance scheduled and executed per plan; review equipment performance data; compare actual performance to strategy targets; confirm adjustments made if performance inadequate

metadata.framework: Control Statement Format: Who | What | When | Where | Why | How | Evidence
metadata.controlIdFormat: AREA-SUBPROCESS-### where AREA is process area abbreviation and SUBPROCESS is activity abbreviation
metadata.notes: Comprehensive extraction of 167 controls from representative SEAM practice files covering core functional areas: 5-Year HAZOP, AIPSM CE/HB/CA Management, AIPSM Reviews and KPIs, Statement of Fitness, ALARP Demonstration, Chemical/OSP Assurance, Causal Learning, Business Continuity, Tank Cleaning, Electrical Safety, Driver Safety, Excavation, Contractor HSSE, Ensure Safe Production, Fatigue Risk Management, Fitness to Work, Hot Work, Lifting & Hoisting, Managing Threats, Dropped Objects Prevention, Knowledge Management, Learning & Improvement, Impact Assessment, Effective Assurance, Manage Reliability, and Managed Asset Care. Each control follows strict 7-element format (Who/What/When/Where/Why/How/Evidence). HSSE-critical controls (62%) prioritized for assurance activities.
metadata.completionStatus: COMPLETE - All 167 controls extracted from representative SEAM practice files and compiled into comprehensive control library
nextSteps: Integration into SEAM Assurance App database
Development of risk-based control testing strategies
Mapping of controls to specific assurance work processes
Training of assurance teams on control framework
Continuous refinement based on field application feedback
